perturbation.original_text: A claimant who has been completely or partially denied a benefit shall be entitled to appeal this denial of his or her claim by filing a written statement of his or her position with the Plan Administrator no later than sixty (60) days after receipt of the written notification of such denial.
perturbation.changed_text: A claimant who has been completely or partially denied a benefit shall be entitled to appeal this denial of his or her claim by filing a written statement of his or her position with the Plan Administrator no later than thirty (30) days after receipt of the written notification of such denial.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the appeal period to 30 days contradicts ERISA regulations that require a minimum of 60 days for appealing denied claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(h)(4) mandates that a claimant be given at least 60 days to appeal a denied claim under an ERISA plan. By shortening the appeal window to 30 days, the modified text violates this federal regulation, potentially denying claimants adequate time to prepare their appeals.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 2560.503-1 - Claims procedure. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 2560.503-1 - Claims procedure. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 2560.503-1 Claims procedure. (a) Scope and purpose. In accordance with the authority of sections 503 and 505 of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 ( ERISA or the Act), 29 U.S.C. 1133 , 1135 , this section sets forth minimum requirements for employee benefit plan procedures pertaining to claims for benefits by participants and beneficiaries (hereinafter referred to as claimants). Except as otherwise specifically provided in this section, these requirements apply to every employee benefit plan described in section 4(a) and not exempted under section 4(b) of the Act. (b) Obligation to establish and maintain reasonable claims procedures. Every employee benefit plan shall establish and maintain reasonable procedures governing the filing of benefit claims, notification of benefit determinations, and appeal of adverse benefit determinations (hereinafter collectively referred to as claims procedures). The claims procedures for a plan will be deemed to be reasonable only if— (1) The claims procedures comply with the requirements of paragraphs (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (h), (i), and (j) of this section, as appropriate, except to the extent that the claims procedures are deemed to comply with some or all of such provisions pursuant to paragraph (b)(6) of this section; (2) A description of all claims procedures (including, in the case of a group health plan within the meaning of paragraph (m)(6) of this section, any procedures for obtaining prior approval as a prerequisite for obtaining a benefit, such as preauthorization procedures or utilization review procedures) and the applicable time frames is included as part of a summary plan description meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 2520.102-3 ; (3) The claims procedures do
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the appeal period from 60 to 30 days, which directly contradicts the ERISA regulation requiring a minimum of 60 days for appealing denied claims. The Perturbation Explanation states this contradiction, and the Law Explanation confirms that 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(h)(4) mandates at least 60 days. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly contain the (h)(4) section, Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally refers to 29 CFR § 2560.503-1 as outlining claims procedure requirements, lending support to the explanation that this CFR section governs appeal timelines. Reducing the appeal window violates this federal regulation, as explained.

perturbation.original_text: This notice of denial of benefits will be provided within ninety (90) days of the Plan Administrator's receipt of the claim for benefits.
perturbation.changed_text: This notice of denial of benefits will be provided within one hundred and twenty (120) days of the Plan Administrator's receipt of the claim for benefits.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of benefits denial to 120 days exceeds the maximum allowable timeframe under ERISA regulations, which require notification within 90 days unless special circumstances exist with proper notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(f)(1) specifies that a plan administrator must notify a claimant of the denial of benefits within a reasonable period, not to exceed 90 days from the receipt of the claim (with a possible extension of up to 180 days under special circumstances if notice is given). By stating the notification will be given in 120 days without noting special circumstances, the changed text exceeds the standard timeframe and fails to adhere to the regulation's stipulations on extensions, contradicting the ERISA regulation.

In accordance with the authority of sections 503 and 505 of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 ( ERISA or the Act), 29 U.S.C. 1133 , 1135 , this section sets forth minimum requirements for employee benefit plan procedures pertaining to claims for benefits by participants and beneficiaries (hereinafter referred to as claimants). Every employee benefit plan shall establish and maintain reasonable procedures governing the filing of benefit claims, notification of benefit determinations, and appeal of adverse benefit determinations (hereinafter collectively referred to as claims procedures). The claims procedures for a plan will be deemed to be reasonable only if— (1) The claims procedures comply with the requirements of paragraphs (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (h), (i), and (j) of this section, as appropriate, except to the extent that the claims procedures are deemed to comply with some or all of such provisions pursuant to paragraph (b)(6) of this section; (2) A description of all claims procedures (including, in the case of a group health plan within the meaning of paragraph (m)(6) of this section, any procedures for obtaining prior approval as a prerequisite for obtaining a benefit, such as preauthorization procedures or utilization review procedures) and the applicable time frames is included as part of a summary plan description meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 2520.102-3 ; (3) The claims procedures do
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the notification timeframe from 90 to 120 days, exceeding the ERISA regulation's specified reasonable period of 90 days for notifying a claimant of benefits denial, as stated in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this exceeds the allowable timeframe. Scraped Law Snippet 2, "29 CFR § 2560.503-1 - Claims procedure," establishes requirements for claims procedures, and the change directly contradicts these requirements by extending the notification period beyond what is generally considered reasonable under ERISA, creating a clear inconsistency with legal obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Plan Administrator shall schedule an opportunity for a full and fair review of the issue within thirty (30) days of receipt of the appeal.
perturbation.changed_text: The Plan Administrator shall schedule an opportunity for a full and fair review of the issue within ninety (90) days of receipt of the appeal.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the review period to 90 days after the appeal exceeds the timeframe allowed without justification under ERISA regulations, potentially causing delays and violating regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(i)(3) indicates that a plan administrator must generally make a benefit determination on review within a reasonable period, and the regulations specify timeframes. By stating that the Plan Administrator shall schedule an opportunity for review of the issue in 90 days, instead of adhering to a timeline more closely aligned with the regulations, the modified text potentially violates ERISA by exceeding the reasonable timeframe for processing appeals and therefore not being in accordance with the applicable timeframe standards. The specific timeframes differ depending on if the appeal is regarding disability, but even under that extended time, the standard circumstances do not cover a 90 day scheduling period. The regulation outlines the requirements of a full and fair review, which includes adherence to appropriate timelines.

The claims procedures for a plan will be deemed to be reasonable only if— (1) The claims procedures comply with the requirements of paragraphs (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (h), (i), and (j) of this section, as appropriate, except to the extent that the claims procedures are deemed to comply with some or all of such provisions pursuant to paragraph (b)(6) of this section; (2) A description of all claims procedures (including, in the case of a group health plan within the meaning of paragraph (m)(6) of this section, any procedures for obtaining prior approval as a prerequisite for obtaining a benefit, such as preauthorization procedures or utilization review procedures) and the applicable time frames is included as part of a summary plan description meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 2520.102-3 ; (3) The claims procedures do
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the review period to 90 days, which contradicts ERISA regulations. The Law Explanation points to 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(i)(3), indicating that benefit determinations must be made within a reasonable period, and the 90-day period exceeds the timeframe for standard circumstances. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing 29 CFR § 2560.503-1, outlines minimum requirements for claims procedures, emphasizing the obligation to establish and maintain reasonable procedures governing benefit claims and appeals. The extension to 90 days goes against the reasonable timeframe standards mandated by ERISA, thus creating a contradiction.
